quiz_questions.question_text: According to Arrhenius, an acid:

options.text: Accepts protons from other molecules
Produces H⁺ ions in water
Donates electrons to other atoms
Produces OH⁻ ions in water

quiz_questions.explanation: According to Arrhenius, an acid is a substance that produces H⁺ (hydrogen) ions when dissolved in water. This definition only applies to aqueous (water-based) solutions.

quiz_questions.question_text: According to Arrhenius, a base:

options.text: Produces H⁺ ions in water
Produces OH⁻ ions in water
Accepts electron pairs from acids
Donates protons to other molecules

quiz_questions.explanation: According to Arrhenius, a base is a substance that produces OH⁻ (hydroxide) ions when dissolved in water. NaOH and KOH are classic examples of Arrhenius bases.

quiz_questions.question_text: According to Bronsted-Lowry, an acid is a:

options.text: Proton (H⁺) donor
Electron pair acceptor
Electron pair donor
Proton (H⁺) acceptor

quiz_questions.explanation: A Bronsted-Lowry acid is a proton (H⁺) donor. This definition is broader than Arrhenius because it does not require water. Any molecule that donates H⁺ acts as a Bronsted-Lowry acid.

quiz_questions.question_text: According to Bronsted-Lowry, a base is a:

options.text: Electron pair acceptor
Proton (H⁺) donor
Electron pair donor
Proton (H⁺) acceptor

quiz_questions.explanation: A Bronsted-Lowry base is a proton (H⁺) acceptor. Any molecule or ion that accepts a proton acts as a Bronsted-Lowry base. This is broader than the Arrhenius definition.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Lewis definition of an acid is:

options.text: A proton acceptor in solution
A proton donor in solution
An electron pair donor
An electron pair acceptor

quiz_questions.explanation: A Lewis acid is an electron pair acceptor. This is the broadest acid-base definition because it includes reactions that do not involve proton (H⁺) transfer at all.

quiz_questions.question_text: NaOH dissolved in water produces:

options.text: H₂ gas and oxygen atoms
Na⁺ and OH⁻ ions
Neutral Na and O atoms
H⁺ and Cl⁻ ions only

quiz_questions.explanation: NaOH is a strong base that completely dissociates in water into Na⁺ (sodium) ions and OH⁻ (hydroxide) ions. The production of OH⁻ ions makes it a base by the Arrhenius definition.

quiz_questions.question_text: HCl dissolved in water produces:

options.text: OH⁻ and Cl⁻ ions only
Only H₂O molecules
NaCl salt precipitate
H⁺ and Cl⁻ ions

quiz_questions.explanation: HCl is a strong acid that completely dissociates in water into H⁺ (hydrogen) ions and Cl⁻ (chloride) ions. The production of H⁺ ions makes it an acid by the Arrhenius definition.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Lewis definition of a base is:

options.text: An electron pair donor
An electron pair acceptor
A proton donor in solution
A producer of H⁺ ions

quiz_questions.explanation: A Lewis base is an electron pair donor. It donates a pair of electrons to form a coordinate covalent bond with a Lewis acid (electron pair acceptor).

quiz_questions.question_text: KOH dissolved in water is classified as:

options.text: An acid because it contains hydrogen
A base because it produces OH⁻ ions
A neutral salt dissolved in water
An acid because it dissociates in water

quiz_questions.explanation: KOH (potassium hydroxide) dissociates into K⁺ and OH⁻ ions in water. Because it produces OH⁻ ions, it is classified as a base by the Arrhenius definition.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is an Arrhenius acid?

options.text: NaOH (sodium hydroxide)
NaCl (sodium chloride)
HNO₃ (nitric acid)
KOH (potassium hydroxide)

quiz_questions.explanation: HNO₃ produces H⁺ ions when dissolved in water, making it an Arrhenius acid. NaOH and KOH are bases (they produce OH⁻), and NaCl is a neutral salt.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the Arrhenius definition of an acid?

options.text: A substance that donates a proton in any solvent
A substance that produces H⁺ ions in aqueous solution
A substance that accepts an electron pair from a base
A substance that produces OH⁻ ions in aqueous solution

quiz_questions.explanation: The Arrhenius definition states that an acid is a substance that produces H⁺ (hydrogen) ions in aqueous (water) solution. This was one of the first scientific definitions of acids.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the Bronsted-Lowry definition of a base?

options.text: A substance that produces OH⁻ ions in water only
A substance that donates a proton to another molecule
A substance that accepts a proton (H⁺) from another molecule
A substance that donates an electron pair to form bonds

quiz_questions.explanation: The Bronsted-Lowry definition says a base is a proton (H⁺) acceptor. This is broader than Arrhenius because it works in any solvent, not just water.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a conjugate acid-base pair?

options.text: Two substances that differ by one proton (H⁺)
Two substances that have the same chemical formula
Two substances that both produce OH⁻ in water
Two substances that neutralize each other completely

quiz_questions.explanation: A conjugate acid-base pair consists of two species that differ by exactly one proton (H⁺). When an acid donates H⁺, it becomes its conjugate base, and vice versa.

quiz_questions.question_text: Define a Lewis acid in terms of electron pairs.

options.text: A species that donates a pair of electrons
A species that accepts a pair of electrons
A species that shares a pair of electrons equally
A species that destroys a pair of electrons

quiz_questions.explanation: A Lewis acid is defined as a species (atom, ion, or molecule) that accepts a pair of electrons. This is the broadest acid definition and does not require protons or water.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'aqueous solution' mean?

options.text: A substance dissolved in an organic solvent
A substance dissolved in water
A substance in a gaseous state at room temperature
A substance that has been heated until it boils

quiz_questions.explanation: Aqueous solution means a substance is dissolved in water. The Arrhenius definition specifically requires aqueous solutions, which is why it is considered the most limited acid-base definition.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the key limitation of the Arrhenius acid-base definition?

options.text: It only applies to reactions that occur in water
It only applies to reactions between two metals
It cannot identify any strong acids or bases
It only works at temperatures above 100 degrees

quiz_questions.explanation: The Arrhenius definition only works for aqueous solutions (dissolved in water). It cannot explain acid-base behavior in non-aqueous solvents or gas-phase reactions, which is why broader definitions were developed.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'amphoteric' mean in acid-base chemistry?

options.text: A substance that is always acidic in every solution
A substance that can act as either an acid or a base
A substance that is neither acidic nor basic ever
A substance that only reacts with strong acids

quiz_questions.explanation: Amphoteric means a substance can act as either an acid or a base depending on what it reacts with. Water is a classic example: it can donate H⁺ (act as acid) or accept H⁺ (act as base).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the definition of a proton in acid-base chemistry?

options.text: A negatively charged subatomic particle
A hydrogen ion (H⁺) with no electrons
A neutral atom with no electrical charge
An electron that has been removed from an atom

quiz_questions.explanation: In acid-base chemistry, a proton refers to a hydrogen ion (H⁺). A hydrogen atom has one proton and one electron; when it loses its electron, only the proton remains, so H⁺ is called a proton.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the difference between the Arrhenius and Bronsted-Lowry definitions of an acid?

options.text: There is no difference between the two definitions
Arrhenius requires water; Bronsted-Lowry works in any solvent
Arrhenius focuses on electrons; Bronsted-Lowry focuses on protons
Bronsted-Lowry only works in water; Arrhenius works everywhere

quiz_questions.explanation: The Arrhenius definition requires water (aqueous solution), while Bronsted-Lowry works in any solvent. Both involve H⁺, but Bronsted-Lowry is broader because it describes H⁺ transfer, not just H⁺ production.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term 'dissociation' mean when applied to acids and bases?

options.text: The process of mixing two solutions together
The process of a compound splitting into ions in water
The process of evaporating water from a solution
The process of freezing a liquid into a solid state

quiz_questions.explanation: Dissociation is the process of a compound separating into its ions when dissolved in water. For acids, this means releasing H⁺ ions; for bases, it means releasing OH⁻ ions.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student dissolves NH₃ (ammonia) in water. It does not contain OH⁻ in its formula, but the solution tests as basic. Which definition best explains why NH₃ is a base?

options.text: Arrhenius, because NH₃ releases OH⁻ directly from its formula
Bronsted-Lowry, because NH₃ accepts a proton (H⁺) from water
Neither definition can explain why NH₃ is a base
Arrhenius, because all nitrogen compounds are bases

quiz_questions.explanation: NH₃ does not contain OH⁻, so Arrhenius cannot easily explain it as a base. However, NH₃ accepts a proton from water (NH₃ + H₂O -> NH₄⁺ + OH⁻), making it a Bronsted-Lowry base.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a lab, a student tests two solutions with litmus paper. Solution X turns blue litmus red, and Solution Y turns red litmus blue. How should she classify them?

options.text: X is a base and Y is an acid based on litmus results
X is an acid and Y is a base based on litmus results
Both X and Y are acids because they changed litmus color
Both X and Y are bases because they changed litmus color

quiz_questions.explanation: Acids turn blue litmus paper red (Solution X is an acid). Bases turn red litmus paper blue (Solution Y is a base). This is the standard litmus test for identifying acids and bases.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student is studying the reaction: HCl + H₂O -> H₃O⁺ + Cl⁻. According to Bronsted-Lowry, which substance is the acid and which is the base?

options.text: HCl is the base because it reacts with water molecules
H₂O is the acid because it gains a proton from HCl
HCl is the acid (donates H⁺) and H₂O is the base (accepts H⁺)
Both HCl and H₂O are acids in this particular reaction

quiz_questions.explanation: HCl donates a proton (H⁺) to water, making HCl the Bronsted-Lowry acid. Water accepts the proton to become H₃O⁺, making water the Bronsted-Lowry base in this reaction.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student finds that a mystery solution has a pH of 7, does not change litmus paper, and does not feel slippery. What is the solution most likely?

options.text: A strong acid that is very dilute in water
A strong base that is very dilute in water
A neutral substance such as pure water
A mixture of a strong acid and a weak base

quiz_questions.explanation: A pH of 7, no litmus change, and no slippery feel all indicate a neutral substance. Pure water is the most common neutral substance with a pH of exactly 7.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student is asked to identify the conjugate base of HNO₃ in this reaction: HNO₃ + H₂O -> H₃O⁺ + NO₃⁻. What is the conjugate base?

options.text: H₂O because it is present before the reaction
H₃O⁺ because it is formed during the reaction
HNO₃ because it is the original acid compound
NO₃⁻ because HNO₃ lost a proton to become it

quiz_questions.explanation: When HNO₃ donates its H⁺ proton, it becomes NO₃⁻ (nitrate). The conjugate base is what remains after an acid loses one proton. HNO₃ and NO₃⁻ are a conjugate acid-base pair.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a lab experiment, a student observes that water acts as a base when reacting with HF, but acts as an acid when reacting with NH₃. What property of water does this demonstrate?

options.text: Water is always an acid in every chemical reaction
Water is always a base in every chemical reaction
Water is amphoteric and can act as an acid or a base
Water is neutral and cannot participate in reactions

quiz_questions.explanation: Water is amphoteric, meaning it can act as either an acid or a base. With HF, water accepts H⁺ (acts as a base). With NH₃, water donates H⁺ (acts as an acid).

quiz_questions.question_text: A student dissolves Ca(OH)₂ in water and tests the solution. She expects it to be basic. Which observation would confirm her prediction?

options.text: Blue litmus paper turns red when dipped in the solution
The solution has a sour taste and reacts with metals
Red litmus paper turns blue and the solution feels slippery
The solution does not change litmus paper either way

quiz_questions.explanation: Ca(OH)₂ is a base that produces OH⁻ ions in water. Bases turn red litmus paper blue and feel slippery. Sour taste and reacting with metals are properties of acids, not bases.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student is comparing the three acid-base definitions. She wants to know which definition is the broadest. Which answer is correct?

options.text: Arrhenius is broadest because it was developed first
Bronsted-Lowry is broadest because it involves protons
Lewis is broadest because it involves electron pairs, not just protons
All three definitions are equally broad in their scope

quiz_questions.explanation: The Lewis definition is the broadest because it defines acids and bases in terms of electron pairs, which applies to many reactions beyond proton transfer. Arrhenius is the most narrow (water only).

quiz_questions.question_text: A student observes that BF₃ (boron trifluoride) has no H⁺ to donate, yet it is classified as an acid. Which definition explains this?

options.text: Arrhenius, because BF₃ produces H⁺ ions in water
Bronsted-Lowry, because BF₃ donates protons to bases
Lewis, because BF₃ accepts an electron pair from a base
None of the definitions can classify BF₃ as an acid

quiz_questions.explanation: BF₃ has no hydrogen to donate, so it cannot be an Arrhenius or Bronsted-Lowry acid. However, BF₃ can accept an electron pair, making it a Lewis acid. This shows the power of the Lewis definition.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student writes three equations and tries to identify the acid-base theory for each. Which matching is correct?

options.text: All three reactions are best explained by Arrhenius only
Arrhenius: H⁺/OH⁻ in water; Bronsted-Lowry: H⁺ transfer; Lewis: electron pairs
Arrhenius: electron pairs; Bronsted-Lowry: H⁺ in water; Lewis: H⁺ transfer
All three definitions are identical and interchangeable

quiz_questions.explanation: Each theory has its own focus: Arrhenius deals with H⁺ and OH⁻ production in water, Bronsted-Lowry deals with proton (H⁺) transfer in any solvent, and Lewis deals with electron pair donation and acceptance.
